coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent sensing of ascorbic acid based on iodine induced oxidative etching and aggregation of lysozyme-templated silver nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Mo, Qinchao
Liu, Fang
Gao, Jing
Zhao, Meiping
Shao, Na
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed a sensitive and highly selective fluorescent approach for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) by taking advantage of the oxidative etching effect of iodine (I2) on the lysozyme-stabilized silver nanoclusters (dLys-AgNCs) with fluorescence quenching. I2 could be produced from the redox reaction between iodate (IO3 −) and AA, and thus the fluorescence intensity of dLys-AgNCs was turned off significantly in the coexistence of IO3 − and AA. The fluorescence quenching of dLys-AgNCs had a good linear relationship with AA concentration, which allowed the detection of AA in the range from 0.05 to 45.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 20 nmol L−1. The quenching mechanism was elucidated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), zeta potential, and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements, confirming that the fluorescence quenching of the dLys-AgNCs was attributed to the oxidative etching of the in situ generated I2, inducing aggregation of the dLys-AgNCs probe by forming Ag@AgI nanocomposite. The dLys-AgNCs probe exhibited excellent selectivity for AA sensing over several common reducing agents tested. Moreover, this approach was extended to the detection of AA in orange juice and urine with recovery rates in the range of 96.0% (RSD: 4.11) to 100.9% (RSD: 3.28) and 94.5% (RSD: 6.40) to 99.2% (RSD: 5.36), respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid (AA)
Silver nanoclusters
Oxidative etching
Iodate (IO3 −)
Iodine (I2)
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence characters of dLys-AgNCs and responses to AA and other substances
The FTIR spectra, zeta potential and DLS measurements
Detection of AA in real samples
Fluorescence characteristics of as-prepared dLys-AgNCs and the optimization of the experimental condition
Sensitive and selective sensing of AA by using dLys-AgNCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The FTIR spectra of (a) AA, and (b) AA after interaction with IO3 −. (B) Proposed structures of AA before and after reactions with IO3 −. (C) XPS spectra in the Ag 3d regions of dLys-AgNCs. (D) XPS results of I 3d for the sample of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − + AA. (E) XPS spectra in the Ag 3d regions of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − + AA.

TEM images of (A) as-prepared dLys-AgNCs, (B) dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) and (C) dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) + AA (10 μmol L−1). (D) DLS measurement of (a) dLys-AgNCs, (b) dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1), and (c) dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) + AA (10 μmol L−1) (The DLS measuring parameters of AgNCs: suspension: water, viscosity: 0.890cp, the refraction coefficient: 1.448).

(A) Fluorescent responses of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − in different concentrations of AA from 0, 0.05, 0.2, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 35.0–45.0 μmol L−1. The arrow indicated the signal changes with increasing AA concentrations. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching degree (F
0
/F) and the concentration of AA. F were inversely proportional to AA concentrations. (C) Fluorescent responses of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − to different species including Fe3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, citric acid, glucose, Gly, Phe, Lysine, Trp, BSA, Lys, VB1, uric acid and AA. The concentration of Fe3+ was 50 μmol L−1, and that of AA was 10 μmol L−1, and all the others were 100 μmol L−1.
Sensing mechanism of dLys-AgNCs for the detection of ascorbic acid.
Comparison of this study with other works for fluorescent detection of AA.
Analytical results (mean ± S; n = 3) of AA in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Our method proposed in this work.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Method accepted universally according to the reported [44].

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Known amount of AA added to the tested sample.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent sensing of ascorbic acid based on iodine induced oxidative etching and aggregation of lysozyme-templated silver nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Peking University, Beijing 100871, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a sensitive and highly selective fluorescent approach for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) by taking advantage of the oxidative etching effect of iodine (I2) on the lysozyme-stabilized silver nanoclusters (dLys-AgNCs) with fluorescence quenching. I2 could be produced from the redox reaction between iodate (IO3 −) and AA, and thus the fluorescence intensity of dLys-AgNCs was turned off significantly in the coexistence of IO3 − and AA. The fluorescence quenching of dLys-AgNCs had a good linear relationship with AA concentration, which allowed the detection of AA in the range from 0.05 to 45.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 20 nmol L−1. The quenching mechanism was elucidated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), zeta potential, and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements, confirming that the fluorescence quenching of the dLys-AgNCs was attributed to the oxidative etching of the in situ generated I2, inducing aggregation of the dLys-AgNCs probe by forming Ag@AgI nanocomposite. The dLys-AgNCs probe exhibited excellent selectivity for AA sensing over several common reducing agents tested. Moreover, this approach was extended to the detection of AA in orange juice and urine with recovery rates in the range of 96.0% (RSD: 4.11) to 100.9% (RSD: 3.28) and 94.5% (RSD: 6.40) to 99.2% (RSD: 5.36), respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), a water-soluble vitamin, is known as a vital antioxidant. It takes part in numerous biological activities as a reducing agent and plays an important role in many biochemical processes [1,2]. AA is a medication for liver disease, scurvy, drug poisoning, allergic reactions and atherosclerosis [3–5]. Furthermore, AA is used as an antioxidant for soft drinks and the manufacture of juices. Considering the important roles of AA, the detection of AA is of substantial importance in the clinical, pharmaceutical and food industries [6,7].

Common analytical methods for the detection of AA, including spectrophotometric [8–10], electrochemical [11–13], and HPLC methods [14,15], as well as chromatographic methods [16], have been widely developed. In addition to the approaches mentioned above, optical sensing methods using fluorescent probes such as quantum dots (QDs) [17–21] and noble metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have been a popular option for the detection of AA in recent years.

MNCs, which possess unique characters such as photostability, ultrafine size, and excellent biocompatibility, have provided clear advantages of simplicity, convenience and rapid implementation for fluorescent sensing. MNCs have been applied for the sensing of AA by many researchers. Malashikhina's group [9] designed an assay for the rapid detection of AA with a DNAzyme cleaving its DNA substrate in the presence of Cu2+ and AA. With good selectivity, the method was applied for rapid and sensitive analysis of AA in vitamin C tablets, urine and orange juice. Wang's group [22] demonstrated a novel strategy for the detection of AA based on the Fenton reaction by DNA-templated silver nanoclusters (DNA-AgNCs), and the approach provided favorable detection limits for AA. Lv's group [23] developed a simple fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of AA with a turn-off mode using protein-modified Au nanoclusters based on the protection of Au (0) from reducing to Au (I) by the control of AA. And Khashab's group [24] developed a simple method for the detection of antioxidants such as AA. The sensing mechanism was also based on the fact that antioxidants can protect the fluorescence of AuNCs against quenching by highly reactive oxygen species. Lin et al. report on the fluorescence quenching of the ultra-small size Au8-cluster with nitroxide radicals and the restoration of the fluorescence when nitroxide radicals were reduced by ascorbic acid (AA), developing a turn-on fluorescence probe for sensing AA in living cells [25].

Long time ago, Mulvaney et al. [26] had examined the effect of molecular iodine on the absorption spectrum of the silica-coated silver nanoparticles (NPs) and revealed that the absorption changes were due to metallic silver oxidation and formation of colloidal AgI. Also, Wang's group [27] had demonstrated that iodine chemisorption could take place on gold NPs, and both aggregation and fusion/fragmentation of NPs were observed. In recent years, Zeng et al. [28] proposed a novel sensing platform for colorimetric detection of I− based on Au@Ag core-shell NPs coupled with copper ion (Cu2+). Cu2+ can oxidize I− into I2, which can sequentially oxidize silver shells to produce AgI, leading to observable color changes from yellow to purple. Chen et al. [29] proposed a plasmonic ELISA based on highly sensitive colorimetric detection of ALP, which was achieved by iodine-mediated etching of gold nanorods (AuNRs). And Chen's group also investigated iodine-mediated etching of AuNRs in the presence of iodate and applied this to the on-site detection of dissolved oxygen and salt-iodine concentration [30]. Huang's group [31] had investigated the etching mechanism of various shapes of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), including nanospheres (AuNSs), nanorods (AuNRs), and nanotrigonalprisms (AuNTs), by iodine on a single-nanoparticle level, and the scattering spectra during the etching process showed obvious blue-shifts with decreasing scattered intensity owing to the oxidation of Au atoms into [AuI2]−.

AgNCs, a new kind of nanomaterials, should undergo similar oxidative etching reaction as AgNPs and AgNRs do in the condition of I2. While, the unique fluorescent property of AgNCs should allow fluorescent detection method and provide higher sensitivity than AgNPs and AgNRs which bear absorption characters. In this work, we reported the oxidative etching effect of iodine on nanoclusters, by which a new strategy for the detection of AA based on the fluorescence quenching of our lysozyme-stabilized silver nanoclusters (dLys-AgNCs) was developed. In the presence of both IO3 − and AA, the in situ generated I2 from the redox reaction between IO3 − and AA could induce oxidative etching and aggregation of the dLys-AgNCs by forming Ag@AgI nanocomposite, and thus quenched the fluorescence intensity of our dLys-AgNCs significantly. This method provided advantages of high sensitivity, selectivity and simple implementation, which was comparable to or better than other methods reported for AA detection. The proposed method allowed quantitative analysis of AA in orange juice and urine samples with highly specific and sensitive responses.

Lysozyme (Lys), citric acid, glycine (Gly), phenylalanine (Phe), lysine, tryptophan (Trp), bovine serum albumin (BSA), vitamin B1 (VB1), uric acid and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Beijing Ding Guo Biotechnology Company. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Tianjin Heowns Biochem Technologies LLC. Acetic acid (CH3COOH), absolute ethyl alcohol (EtOH), sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3), glucose and sodium acetate (CH3COONa) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. Potassium iodate (KIO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 96%), iodine (I2) and other reagents were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. All stock solutions were prepared with ultrapure water which was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system and had an electrical resistivity above 18.0 MΩ cm−1.

Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The FTIR spectra were recorded on an IRA ffinity-1 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed with a Brookhaven zeta potential analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was conducted on a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a TF 20 (FEI) microscope. The pH of the buffer solution was adjusted by a HANNA HI 2211 pH/ORP meter. The emission spectra were recorded over the wavelength range of 500–700 nm upon excitation at 365 nm. Each spectrum was the average of three scans.

Silver nanoclusters capped with denatured lysozyme (dLys-AgNCs) were prepared according to our previous report [32]. Briefly, 5 mL of lysozyme (15 mg mL−1) solution was introduced into a freshly prepared AgNO3 (5 mmol L−1) solution (1 mL), and vigorously stirred for 5 min at room temperature. The molar ratio of AgNO3 and lysozyme was approximately 1:1. Then, 0.3 mL of NaOH (1 mol L−1) was added dropwise to the mixed solution mentioned above. After stirring for 5 min, 10 μL of NaBH4 (20 mmol L−1) was injected. Then, the solution was vigorously stirred in the dark at room temperature for 15 min. To obtain stable dLys-AgNCs, 0.5 mL of acetic acid (1 mol L−1) was added to neutralize excess NaOH. The mixture was then stirred again for 5 min. The dLys-AgNCs were obtained. The final solution was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characters of dLys-AgNCs and responses to AA and other substances
ce:section.ce:para: For fluorescence spectra measurements of dLys-AgNCs, the stock solutions of dLys-AgNCs were used. For the respective fluorescence responses of dLys-AgNCs to IO3 − and AA, the probes of dLys-AgNCs were diluted 20 times. For measuring the fluorescence responses of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − to AA and other substances including reducing reagents, amino acids and proteins, the probes were diluted 20 times, the concentration of Fe3+ was 50 μmol L−1, and that of AA was 10 μmol L−1, and all the others were 100 μmol L−1. For AA detection, 40 μL of as-prepared dLys-AgNCs was diluted with sodium acetate-acetic acid buffer solution (40 μL, 10 mmol L−1, pH 4.8), followed by the addition of 0.2 mL of IO3 − (4 mmol L−1) and different concentrations of AA, and the solution was diluted with ultrapure water to a final volume of 800 μL. After incubation at room temperature for 25 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were measured with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FTIR spectra, zeta potential and DLS measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of AA alone and AA with IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) were recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using KBr pellets. For zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements, dLys-AgNCs were diluted 20 times from their stock solutions in the absence or presence of IO3 − and IO3 − + AA.

As-prepared dLys-AgNCs and dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − + AA were dried in a drying oven at 30 °C for 8 h, and then in a vacuum drying oven for 3 days. After tableting, the X-ray photoelectron spectra of dLys-AgNCs and dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − + AA were measured. The XPS spectra were deconvoluted by XPS PEAK 41.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Orange juice and human urine were used for the detection of AA concentration in real samples. The urine sample was collected from a healthy adult male, and orange juice was purchased from the supermarket. The orange juice and urine samples were first filtered by Pall filter, and the filtrates were collected. The orange juice and urine filtrates were diluted using sodium acetate-acetic acid buffer solution (10 mmol L−1, pH 4.8) 100- and 60- times, respectively. The test samples were prepared by adding different concentrations of AA to the real samples. The fluorescence intensity at 640 nm was measured, and the detected concentration was calculated according to the linear equation of the dLys-AgNCs. The recovery was the ratio of the detected concentration (Found) to the added concentration (Added).

Iodine-mediated oxidative etching of Au/Ag NPs and NRs had been studied in previous reports [26–31], either on the etching mechanism or the sensing application. And we deem that metal NCs should undergo oxidative etching by iodine as metal NPs and NRs.

As we all know, IO3 − could be reduced by AA with the production of I2. And thus, fluorescent sensing of AA could be achieved based on the oxidative etching of in situ produced I2 on metal NCs with fluorescence quenching via the redox reaction between IO3 − and AA in acid medium. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the coexistence of IO3 − and AA could quench the fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs, while IO3 − or AA alone did not affect the fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs. The in situ produced I2 from the redox reaction between IO3 − and AA could induce oxidative etching and aggregation of the dLys-AgNCs by forming Ag@AgI nanocomposite, thus turning off the fluorescence of the dLys-AgNCs. As shown in Fig. S1A, in the control experiment, AA or IO3 − only showed a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of dLys-AgNCs (Fig. S1A (b and c)) compared with that of dLys-AgNCs itself (Fig. S1A (a)), while in the presence of both IO3 − and AA, the fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs was remarkably quenched (Fig. S1A (d)). This specific fluorescent response of dLys-AgNCs to IO3 − and AA can be engineered into a novel fluorescent sensor for AA detection.

Moreover, the effect of adding order of IO3 − and AA on the fluorescence changes of dLys-AgNCs was investigated. As shown in Fig.S1B, when IO3 − was added first (line a and c), the fluorescence intensities were a bit lower than those when AA was added first (line b and d). It was deemed that IO3 − might have very weak oxidation effect on the dLys-AgNCs probe and quench the fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs as the result shown in Fig.S1A(c). Compared with the oxidative etching effect of the in situ produced I2, the effect of IO3 − on the fluorescence changes of dLys-AgNCs was negligible. And in the following experiment for studying the responses of dLys-AgNCs to AA in the presence of IO3 −, IO3 −was added first all the way.

The sensing mechanism of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − for AA was explored. In our experiment, the concentration of IO3 − was much higher than that of AA, and the overall reactions of the sensing system were primarily as following:
(1) 2IO3 − + 5C6H8O6 + 2H+ → 5C6H6O6 + I2 + 6H2O
(2) 2Ag + I2 → 2AgI↓

AA could reduce IO3 − to I−, and then I− was oxidized to I2 by excessive IO3 −, and subsequently I2 induced the oxidative etching and aggregation of the dLys-AgNCs by forming Ag@AgI nanocomposite due to the low Ksp of AgI (8.52 × 10−17, 25 °C). To verify the validity of our assumption, the following experiments were performed.

The FTIR spectra of AA alone and AA with IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) were shown in Fig. 1 A. In Fig. 1A, the strong bands in the region of 3220–3528 cm−1 were attributed to the OH stretching due to the presence of moisture in the sample. As shown in Fig. 1A (a), the observed frequency at 1670 cm−1 should be correlated to the v (C=C) mode and the frequency at 1759 cm−1 was correlated to the lactone carbonyl v (C=O) mode, respectively. The frequency at 1300 cm−1 was ascribed to the enolic v (O-H) mode and that at 1185 cm−1 was attributed to the branched chin v (O-H) mode [33]. When IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) was added, as shown in Fig. 1A (b), the strong absorption peak of v (C=C) at 1670 cm−1 disappeared, and the peaks at 1796 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 which were correlated to the lactone v (C=O) mode and the conjugated v (C=O) mode emerged, implying the changes of enol to ketone. The strong band at 1300 cm−1 originated from enolic hydroxyl bending vanished. These results indicated that AA should be oxidized by IO3 −, and the corresponding structure changes were shown in Fig. 1B.

To confirm the production of I2 from the reactions between IO3 − and AA, the concentrations of IO3 − and AA were both increased 50×. We could clearly observe that the solution become pale yellow after mixing IO3 − (50 mmol L−1) and AA (500 μmol L−1), and the starch solution immediately became blue when the pale yellow solution was injected as shown in Fig. S2. We also investigated the effect of I2 on the fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs. The fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs at 640 nm decreased as the I2 concentration increased, and F
0
/F was linear with the incremental concentrations of I2 as shown in Fig. S3. In addition, we found that when the fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs was significantly quenched, the maximal dosage ratio of I2 (Fig. S3) to AA (Fig. 3A) was close to 1:5 (10:45), which was coincident with our proposed overall reactions of eq. (1).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to determine the valence state of Ag and I in the samples. For dLys-AgNCs, two intense peaks were observed at 368.2 and 374.3 eV (Fig. 1C), with an energy gap of nearly 6.0 eV. The two peaks were assigned to the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 features of Ag (0), respectively, and this was consistent with our previous study [34]. The BE positions of I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 ascribed to IO3 − were at 623.6 and 635.5 eV, respectively [35], as shown in Fig. 1D. For dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − + AA, the binding energy of Ag 3d which appeared at 367.6 (Ag 3d5/2) and 373.9 eV (Ag 3d3/2) (Fig. 1E), confirmed the generation of Ag(I) [36]. The newly formed peaks of I 3d5/2 at 619.0 eV (Fig. 1D) and Ag 3d5/2 peaks at 367.6 eV (Fig. 1E) matched well to the literature values of 619.4 (I 3d5/2) and 368.0 eV (Ag 3d5/2) for pure AgI [37], which was reasonable to presume that AgI was formed after the reaction between dLys-AgNCs and IO3 − + AA.

Furthermore, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology and size of the as-prepared dLys-AgNCs, dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) and dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) + AA (10 μmol L−1). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the as-prepared dLys-AgNCs were well dispersed and mostly spherical. In Fig. 2B, the average particle sizes of dLys-AgNCs when adding IO3 − were slightly larger than that of the original dLys-AgNCs, and this might be due to the very weak oxidation effect of IO3 − on dLys-AgNCs by forming small amount of AgI on the particle in the experimental condition. But notable irregular aggregates were observed after addition of IO3 − + AA to dLys-AgNCs, as shown in Fig. 2C. And this verified our conjecture that the fluorescence quenching of the dLys-AgNCs was ascribable to the aggregation of the probe.

In addition, the zeta potentials and hydrodynamic diameters of dLys-AgNCs, dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) and dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) + AA (10 μmol L−1) were also measured. As showed in Fig. S4, the as-prepared dLys-AgNCs was electrically neutral in the absence and presence of IO3 − or IO3 − + AA, indicating that IO3 − or IO3 − + AA had no effect on their electrical properties. The dynamic light scattering measurement showed that the particle sizes of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (Fig. 2D (b)) were slightly larger than those of the original dLys-AgNCs (Fig. 2D (a)), which was consistent with the TEM result. While the particle size of the dLys-AgNCs were much larger after the addition of IO3 − + AA (Fig. 2D (c)), which further verified that the dLys-AgNCs should undergo aggregation when IO3 − and AA were both in presence.

In all, the in situ produced I2 from the redox reaction between IO3 − and AA was able to induce oxidative etching and aggregation of the dLys-AgNCs by forming Ag@AgI nanocomposite, thus turning off the fluorescence of the dLys-AgNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characteristics of as-prepared dLys-AgNCs and the optimization of the experimental condition
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previous report [32,34], as shown in Fig. S5, the dLys-AgNCs had two excitation wavelengths, 365 nm and 490 nm. Two emission peaks centered at 445 nm and 640 nm appeared when excited at 365 nm. And only one emission peak centered at 640 nm emerged when excited at 490 nm. The emission centered at 445 nm was attributed to the ligand of lysozyme, and the emission at 640 nm belonged to dLys-AgNCs [38,39].
To achieve the best sensing performance, the effects of incubation time, IO3 − concentration and pH on the fluorescence responses of dLys-AgNCs to AA were investigated.

The time-dependent fluorescence changes of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − upon addition of AA (10 μmol L−1) was monitored at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of dLys-AgNCs at 640 nm was quenched greatly in the first 15 min, and reached equilibrium after 25 min. Thus, 25 min were chosen as the optimum incubation time in all the following experiments.

The effect of IO3 − concentration on the responses of dLys-AgNCs to AA was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of dLys-AgNCs achieved a plateau when the IO3 − concentrations were over 1 mmol L−1, which was therefore chosen as the optimum concentration in further study. When the concentration of IO3 − was lower than 1 mmol L−1, the responses of dLys-AgNCs were weaker, the reason of which might be that AA inadequately reacted with IO3 − leading to the weak fluorescent response of dLys-AgNCs.

pH played an important role in the sensing system because it can affect both the fluorescence intensity of dLys-AgNCs as well as the redox reaction between IO3 − and AA. As shown in Fig. S8A, the fluorescence intensity of sole AgNCs changed gently under different pHs studied. And the fluorescence intensity of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − (1 mmol L−1) (Fig. S8B) kept almost unchanged under weak acid and near-neutral conditions but decreased sharply under medium strong acid condition. While the fluorescence quenching degree, F
0
/F (F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − in the absence and presence of AA, respectively), of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − in the presence of AA (10 μmol L−1) (Fig. S8C) changed dramatically when the pH varied in the range of 2.8–7.8, and the maximum response signal was achieved at pH 4.8 which was chosen throughout the following work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective sensing of AA by using dLys-AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimal conditions, the dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − had good fluorescent response to AA. The fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs gradually decreased as the concentration of AA increased from 0, 0.05, 0.2, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 35.0–45.0 μmol L−1, as shown in Fig. 3 A. The fluorescence quenching degree (F
0
/F) versus the AA concentration from 0.05 to 45.0 μmol L−1 was plotted in Fig. 3B, which showed a good linear correlation:
F
0 /F = 0.13[AA] (μmol L−1) + 1.077

That was, F were inversely proportional to AA concentrations. The linear correlation coefficient was 0.9939 and the detection limit of AA was 20 nmol L−1 (S/N = 3), which was comparable to or better than some other fluorescent approaches (see Table 1 ). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.7% for six repeated measurements of 10 μmol L−1 AA.

To test the selective response of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − to AA, the fluorescence changes of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − in the presence of some other species such as metal ions, anions, proteins and reducing agents were tested. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence quenching of dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − by AA was strikingly larger than that by the other substances, indicating that dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − possessed a highly selective response toward AA. Together with the above results, we can conclude that our dLys-AgNCs + IO3 − probe offer a simple, sensitive and selective approach for AA detection.

The proposed method for AA sensing by using dLys-AgNCs as fluorescent probe was preliminarily applied to the detection of AA concentration in orange juice and urine. As shown in Table 2 , it showed that the proposed method found 4.86 μmol AA in 100 times diluted orange juice and 0.61 μmol AA in 60 times diluted urine. The results were essentially agreement with those obtained by the standard method (5.01 μmol AA in diluted orange juice and 0.57 μmol AA in diluted urine) [44], which indicated our proposed method for AA detection had comparative performance to that of the standard method. The recovery experiments were also carried out and satisfactory recoveries were obtained. The added recovery of AA in orange juice were in the range of 96.0% (RSD: 4.11) to 100.9% (RSD: 3.28), and the added recovery rates in urine were in the range of 94.5% (RSD: 6.40) to 99.2% (RSD: 5.36), as shown in Table 2. These demonstrated the feasibility of the dLys-AgNCs probe for quantitative analysis of AA in real samples.

In summary, fluorescent sensing of AA was achieved with high selectivity and sensitivity using dLys-AgNCs as a fluorescent probe. The sensing mechanism was making use of I2 induced oxidative etching and aggregation of the dLys-AgNCs. This dLys-AgNCs probe had high selectivity for AA sensing; other reducing agents such as citric acid, uric acid, glucose and vitamin B1 had no substantive effect on the fluorescence of dLys-AgNCs. Additionally, our dLys-AgNCs fluorescent probe was applied successfully for the detection of AA in orange juice and urine samples, which might provide new insight into the potential application of metal nanoclusters in biosensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
supplementary files supplementary files

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.11.068.
